batches.expectedOutcome: Supported non-interactive execution paths are more coherent from shim to CLI to daemon, and the docs describe those paths precisely.
batches.goal: Make the actual shim/daemon execution path more usable and explicit for supported scenarios, especially around PowerShell command routing, session flow, and installation/status behavior.
batches.name: Batch 2 - Shim and daemon usability completion
verification: manual CLI and shim smoke tests for supported exec flows on the current platform

batches.expectedOutcome: The security story becomes less scaffolded and more behaviorally verified where feasible.
batches.goal: Expand the next realistic enforcement slices beyond current prechecks and Windows job control while preserving honest claims and good tests.
batches.name: Batch 3 - Enforcement depth and policy-backed behavior

batches.expectedOutcome: Cross-platform install and release guidance become defensible against the current runtime maturity.
batches.goal: Tighten install, Docker, CI, and release behavior so repo guidance matches real support.
batches.name: Batch 4 - Cross-platform and packaging truthfulness
verification: dotnet publish checks for documented RIDs

batches.expectedOutcome: The project ends in an honestly reviewable state with explicit remaining blockers if full spec fulfillment is still not reached.
batches.goal: Reassess remaining gaps against request.task.md and either implement the last high-impact items or reduce claims to the exact truth.
batches.name: Batch 5 - Final parity closure
targets: remaining code hotspots identified by earlier batches
verification: full solution build and test run
manual end-to-end validation of the supported product surface

rationale: Batch 1 is complete enough to remove from the front of the queue.
The parity score only moved from 58 to 66, which means the next work must target the actual runtime usability gap rather than more surface cleanup.
CLI reference and stronger shim/daemon execution validation became more urgent because they directly affect whether the project can be used as instructed.
risks: The biggest remaining parity gaps are now concentrated in runtime behavior, not docs, so future batches are likely to surface deeper architectural limitations.
Shim and daemon end-to-end behavior may require changing user-facing boundaries rather than just adding code.
Some platform-native enforcement goals may still need to be documented as future work even after additional implementation.

backlogSeed: Batch 1: Spec and documentation alignment plus integration-test hardening around the currently implemented CLI/daemon/shim surface.
Batch 2: Usability-focused execution path completion for shim and daemon orchestration, including clearer boundaries for supported interactive and non-interactive behaviors.
Batch 3: Deeper enforcement work, prioritizing the most realistic next OS-level controls and policy-backed behavior that can actually be verified in tests.
Batch 4: Cross-platform support tightening, including Linux/macOS fallback behavior, platform capability reporting, and honest packaging/runtime docs.
Batch 5: Broader parity review against request.task.md and docs/architecture.md, removing or implementing any remaining high-impact mismatches.
implementedAreas: CLI commands for exec, session, checkpoint, config, network check, report, status, start, and stop now exercise real code paths rather than placeholder payloads.
Policy loading and first-match-wins evaluation for command, file, network, and environment rules are implemented with automated coverage.
Session persistence, checkpoint persistence, report generation, approval flows, authentication modes, LLM proxy routing, and MCP inspection all have concrete repository implementations with tests.
Hosted PowerShell execution for inline commands now exists via a constrained runspace path, and native process launches have a first OS-level Windows Job Object control.
missingAreas: Usable shim installation and drop-in PATH replacement flow matching the stated agentsh-style usage is not complete.
True platform-native sandbox enforcement on Linux and macOS is missing, and Windows enforcement is only partially started.
End-to-end daemon plus shim integration remains thin, with integration coverage still limited and one integration test still named Placeholder_Integration_Test.
Documentation still contains explicit work-in-progress framing and parity claims that are broader than the verified runtime behavior.
Spec-complete cross-platform behavioral testing, especially real Docker and OS-matrix execution of the product surface, is not yet matched by product-level runtime maturity.
partialAreas: PowerShell execution is usable for explicit inline commands, but interactive shell sessions remain intentionally unsupported and full shell replacement behavior is not complete.
Network enforcement exists as policy evaluation plus explicit pre-launch target blocking, but not as true OS-level egress interception across platforms.

Cross-platform support exists at the project layout, docs, build, and packaging level, but Linux and macOS platform enforcers remain mostly plan/scaffold implementations instead of runtime-complete enforcement layers.
Daemon lifecycle exists for local state tracking and IPC hosting, but the broader architecture described in docs/architecture.md still overstates current transport and platform capabilities.
prioritizedGaps: Close the largest usability gap between the documented agentsh-like model and the actual executable product surface, especially shim/daemon execution flow and unsupported shell interaction boundaries.
Bring docs and packaging claims back into alignment with verified behavior while the implementation is improved, so the project is honest and testable.
Strengthen integration and end-to-end tests around daemon, shim, hosted PowerShell execution, policy enforcement, and blocked-vs-allowed scenarios.
Implement the next real platform-native enforcement slices beyond Windows Job Objects, especially Linux/macOS process and network controls or explicit constrained fallbacks.
Reduce remaining placeholder/scaffold areas in daemon and platform projects, starting with the pieces that directly affect user-facing trust and security expectations.

instructions: Choose the next highest-priority unfinished batch from the backlog.
Implement the code, tests, docs, scripts, and configuration changes required for that batch.
Prefer real behavior over additional scaffolding or placeholders.
Run the relevant local verification commands after making changes and include the results in the summary.
If the batch reveals a narrower root cause than expected, solve that root cause rather than papering over symptoms.
Return exactly what was changed, which files were touched, and what remains from the batch.
prompt.outputFormat: JSON with selectedBatch, filesChanged, commandsRun, outcomes, remainingWithinBatch, and blockers
prompt.role: senior .NET systems engineer executing a prioritized implementation batch
prompt.task: Implement the next highest-priority backlog batch directly in the repository and leave it in a buildable, testable state
